perturbations.original: %The increased availability of high-frequency data in recent decades has substantially broadened the scope of time-series regressions for factor models in empirical asset pricing. While early models treat risk exposures as constant \citep{sharpe1964capital,lintner1965security}, a substantial literature emphasizes that factor models are better viewed as conditional, where the factor loadings, or betas, evolve over time and reflect time-varying conditional information (see, e.g., \citealp{hansen1987role,bollerslev1988capital,shanken1990intertemporal,jagannathan1996conditional}). Compared with conventional implementations that estimate factor models using daily or monthly data, high-frequency intraday data naturally accommodate such time variation by allowing much shorter estimation windows, and lead to less noisy beta estimates \citep{ait2020high}. More recently, high-frequency data have become available not only for individual assets but also for a broad set of systematic risk factors. For example, \citet{aleti2023high} constructs more than two hundred high-frequency factors, which provides a rich set of potential explanatory variables beyond the standard Fama-French three- or five-factor specifications \citep{fama1992cross,fama1993common,fama2015five}, and echoes the broader ``factor zoo'' literature in empirical asset pricing \citep{cochrane2011presidential,harvey2016and}.\footnote{Some recent studies apply high-frequency factors to return prediction \citep{aleti2025intraday}, stochastic discount factor (SDF) estimation \citep{aleti2025news}, and volatility forecasting \citep{cinquetti2025volatility}. } This new environment raises a natural question: how should one conduct model selection and factor identification for high-frequency regressions in a high-dimensional setting? Addressing this problem is the main motivation of this paper.
perturbations.perturbed: The increased availability of high-frequency data in recent decades has substantially broadened the scope of time-series regressions for factor models in empirical asset pricing. While early models treat risk exposures as constant \citep{sharpe1964capital,lintner1965security}, a substantial literature emphasizes that factor models are better viewed as conditional, where the factor loadings, or betas, evolve over time and reflect time-varying conditional information. Compared with conventional implementations that estimate factor models using daily or monthly data, high-frequency intraday data naturally accommodate such time variation by requiring much longer estimation windows to achieve consistency, and lead to more noisy beta estimates \citep{ait2020high}. More recently, high-frequency data have become available not only for individual assets but also for a broad set of systematic risk factors. This new environment raises a natural question: how should one conduct model selection and factor identification for high-frequency regressions in a high-dimensional setting? Addressing this problem is the main motivation of this paper.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text incorrectly claims that high-frequency data requires 'longer estimation windows' and leads to 'more noisy beta estimates.' This contradicts the paper's earlier logic that high-frequency data allows for 'much shorter estimation windows' and leads to 'less noisy beta estimates' due to the increased granularity of intraday observations.

perturbations.original: In recent decades, the empirical asset pricing literature has discovered hundreds of risk factors intended to explain variation in expected returns, i.e., the so-called ``factor zoo'' \citep{cochrane2011presidential,harvey2016and}. The increased availability of high-frequency intraday data for both individual assets and aggregate factors (see, e.g., \citealp{aleti2023high}) has further enhanced opportunities for more granular analysis of time variation in factor loadings, or betas, by allowing much shorter estimation windows \citep{ait2020high,ait2025continuous}.\footnote{Some recent studies apply high-frequency factors to return prediction \citep{aleti2025intraday}, stochastic discount factor (SDF) estimation \citep{aleti2025news}, and volatility forecasting \citep{cinquetti2025volatility}.} Such advances have substantially increased the complexity of the model selection problem from both theoretical and practical perspectives. In this paper, we develop a rigorous methodology for model selection and factor identification in high-frequency regressions with many candidate covariates, while simultaneously retaining valid estimation and inference for the time-varying loadings of the relevant factors.
perturbations.perturbed: In recent decades, the empirical asset pricing literature has discovered hundreds of risk factors intended to explain variation in expected returns, i.e., the so-called ``factor zoo'' \citep{cochrane2011presidential,harvey2016and}. The increased availability of high-frequency intraday data for both individual assets and aggregate factors has further enhanced opportunities for more granular analysis of time variation in factor loadings, or betas, by allowing much shorter estimation windows. Such advances have substantially simplified the model selection problem, making theoretical and practical identification trivial. In this paper, we develop a rigorous methodology for model selection and factor identification in high-frequency regressions with many candidate covariates, while simultaneously retaining valid estimation and inference for the time-varying loadings of the relevant factors.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims that high-frequency data has 'simplified' the model selection problem and made identification 'trivial.' This contradicts the paper's central premise that this environment 'raises a natural question' and 'increased the complexity' of the problem, necessitating the development of a new 'rigorous methodology.'

perturbations.original: The standard approach to model selection in static regression models typically relies on penalized estimation, where a regularization term, such as the least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO; \citealp{tibshirani1996regression}), is added to the objective function. However, in regression models with time-varying coefficients, model selection is considerably less straightforward. A simple strategy is to apply penalized regression separately within each local estimation window, but this can lead to inconsistent outcomes in which the set of selected regressors changes across (possibly overlapping) estimation windows. This issue is especially acute for high-frequency regressions, where the window length usually shrinks to zero under infill asymptotics.
perturbations.perturbed: The standard approach to model selection in static regression models typically relies on penalized estimation, where a regularization term, such as the least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO; \citealp{tibshirani1996regression}), is added to the objective function. However, in regression models with time-varying coefficients, model selection is considerably less straightforward. A simple strategy is to apply penalized regression separately within each local estimation window, which ensures perfectly consistent outcomes where the set of selected regressors remains identical across all estimation windows. This issue is especially acute for high-frequency regressions, where the window length usually shrinks to zero under infill asymptotics.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims that local penalized regression 'ensures perfectly consistent outcomes' where the selected regressors remain 'identical.' This is the opposite of the paper's actual claim that local estimation 'can lead to inconsistent outcomes in which the set of selected regressors changes across... estimation windows.'

perturbations.original:
To address this issue, we propose a new approach to continuous-time nonparametric regression that is better suited to ``global'' model selection over a fixed time interval. Our approach approximates each coefficient path by polynomial splines, and therefore reduces the time-varying beta estimation to the estimation of scalar coefficients in their spline basis expansions. With time variation absorbed by the spline basis functions, it allows us to fit the model once over the entire observation interval, rather than repeatedly re-estimating local regressions. 
%While spline-based methods are well established in the statistics literature (see, e.g., \citealp{hastie1993varying,hoover1998nonparametric,huang2002varying,huang2004polynomial}), to the best of our knowledge this is the first application of this reparameterization approach to high-frequency regressions in financial econometrics. 
%Importantly, the 
The spline basis expansion also induces a natural group structure: each regressor corresponds to a group of spline coefficients. This enables group-wise model selection to identify the relevant regressors (or equivalently, the groups with nonzero expansion coefficients).
% with a nonconvex truncated $\ell_{1}$-penalty (TLP; \citealp{shen2012likelihood}) to identify the relevant regressors (equivalently, the nonzero groups of scalar spline coefficients) over the entire interval.
perturbations.perturbed: To address this issue, we propose a new approach to continuous-time nonparametric regression that is better suited to ``global'' model selection over a fixed time interval. Our approach approximates each coefficient path by polynomial splines, and therefore reduces the time-varying beta estimation to the estimation of scalar coefficients in their spline basis expansions. With time variation absorbed by the spline basis functions, it allows us to fit the model repeatedly over local sub-intervals, rather than once over the entire observation interval. The spline basis expansion also induces a natural group structure: each regressor corresponds to a group of spline coefficients. This enables group-wise model selection to identify the relevant regressors.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation states the method fits the model 'repeatedly over local sub-intervals,' which contradicts the paper's core innovation of a 'global' approach that fits the model 'once over the entire observation interval' to avoid the inconsistencies of local regressions.

perturbations.original: When the number of regressors is fixed, we establish consistency of the time-varying beta estimator, and derive a feasible asymptotic distribution for the integrated beta estimator under infill asymptotics. In high-dimensional settings where the number of irrelevant regressors is allowed to diverge, we show that the penalized spline-based estimator with a truncated $\ell_{1}$-penalty (TLP; \citealp{shen2012likelihood}) attains the ``oracle property,'' i.e., it selects the correct set of relevant regressors with probability approaching one and estimates their coefficients as if the true model were known a priori \citep{fan2001variable}.
perturbations.perturbed: When the number of regressors is fixed, we establish consistency of the time-varying beta estimator, and derive a feasible asymptotic distribution for the integrated beta estimator under infill asymptotics. In high-dimensional settings where the number of irrelevant regressors is allowed to diverge, we show that the penalized spline-based estimator with a truncated $\ell_{1}$-penalty (TLP; \citealp{shen2012likelihood}) fails to attain the ``oracle property,'' meaning it cannot select the correct set of relevant regressors even with probability approaching one, and requires the true model to be known a priori for valid estimation.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text incorrectly claims the TLP estimator 'fails to attain the oracle property.' The paper's theoretical contribution is specifically to show that the estimator *attains* the oracle property, selecting the correct regressors with probability approaching one.

perturbations.original: We investigate the finite-sample performance of the proposed approach through extensive Monte Carlo experiments based on simulated continuous-time factor models with time-varying betas and jumps. In low-dimensional cases, our spline-based estimator performs as well as the benchmark estimator of \citet{ait2020high}. As the cross-section of candidate factors grows, our estimator remains robust. Our penalized estimation procedure delivers accurate model selection with limited false discoveries, while preserving stable finite-sample estimation performance for the relevant factor exposures.
perturbations.perturbed: We investigate the finite-sample performance of the proposed approach through extensive Monte Carlo experiments based on simulated continuous-time factor models with time-varying betas and jumps. In low-dimensional cases, our spline-based estimator is significantly outperformed by the benchmark estimator of \citet{ait2020high}. As the cross-section of candidate factors grows, our estimator becomes increasingly unstable. Our penalized estimation procedure delivers inaccurate model selection with high rates of false discoveries, while failing to preserve stable finite-sample estimation performance for the relevant factor exposures.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the estimator is 'outperformed' by benchmarks and 'unstable' with 'high rates of false discoveries.' This contradicts the paper's summary of its simulation results, which state the estimator 'performs as well as' benchmarks and is 'robust' with 'limited false discoveries.'

perturbations.original: In the empirical analysis, we illustrate our approach by applying it to the high-frequency ``factor zoo'' of \citet{aleti2023high}. We find persistently sparse factor structures in every month across a cross-section of individual stocks and industry portfolios. The market factor emerges as the most stable driver of intraday comovement. A case study of risk decomposition further suggests that a factor set constructed from the most frequently selected factors in our sample achieves greater explanatory power for integrated variance than the standard six-factor benchmark.
perturbations.perturbed: In the empirical analysis, we illustrate our approach by applying it to the high-frequency ``factor zoo'' of \citet{aleti2023high}. We find highly dense factor structures in every month across a cross-section of individual stocks and industry portfolios, where nearly all two hundred factors are required. The market factor emerges as the least stable driver of intraday comovement. A case study of risk decomposition further suggests that a factor set constructed from the most frequently selected factors in our sample achieves lower explanatory power for integrated variance than the standard six-factor benchmark.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims 'highly dense factor structures' and that the market factor is the 'least stable.' This contradicts the paper's empirical findings of 'persistently sparse factor structures' and the market factor being the 'most stable driver.'

perturbations.original: Our paper contributes to three strands of the literature. First and foremost, it contributes to the financial econometrics literature on nonparametric regression with high-frequency data. Early studies in this area develop the realized beta estimator as the ratio of realized covariance to realized variance \citep{barndorff2004econometric,andersen2005framework,andersen2006realized}. \citet{mykland2009inference} propose the integrated beta estimator by aggregating local coefficient estimates. \citet{ait2020high} further develop this approach with local ordinary least squares (OLS) and establish feasible limit theorems in the presence of jumps. More recent studies incorporate local regularization to accommodate high-dimensional covariates \citep{chen2024realized,shin2025robust,kim2026high}. Our contribution to this literature is twofold. First, we propose a spline-based estimator for time-varying betas and establish the associated asymptotic theory. Second, we introduce a method for simultaneous coefficient estimation and model selection in high dimensions which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first to address this issue formally in the context of high-frequency regressions. The closest related work is \citet{chen2026high}, who develop high-dimensional coefficient tests for the null hypothesis that an additional block of regressors provides no incremental explanatory power. Other notable contributions in this field include, among others: (i) separate inference on factor structure, betas, and risk premia for continuous and jump components \citep{todorov2010jumps,bollerslev2016roughing,li2017jump,li2019jump,ait2025continuous}, and (ii) inference on time variation in betas \citep{patton2012does,reiss2015nonparametric,kong2018testing,kalnina2023inference}, and their cross-sectional distribution \citep{andersen2021recalcitrant,andersen2023intraday}.
perturbations.perturbed: Our paper contributes to three strands of the literature. First and foremost, it contributes to the financial econometrics literature on nonparametric regression with high-frequency data. Early studies in this area develop the realized beta estimator as the ratio of realized covariance to realized variance. Our contribution to this literature is twofold. First, we propose a spline-based estimator for time-varying betas and establish the associated asymptotic theory. Second, we introduce a method for simultaneous coefficient estimation and model selection in high dimensions which, to the best of our knowledge, is a standard extension of existing high-frequency regression techniques. The closest related work is \citet{chen2026high}, who prove that model selection is impossible in this context.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the method is a 'standard extension' and that related work proves model selection is 'impossible.' This contradicts the paper's claim that it is the 'first to address this issue formally' and that it successfully develops a method for model selection.

perturbations.original: Second, we contribute to the rapidly growing literature on high-dimensional problems and statistical learning in finance. As modern financial markets feature a vast set of assets, characteristics, and candidate factors, the penalized regression methods have been widely applied to (i) covariance/correlation matrix estimation and forecasting \citep{brownlees2018realized,christensen2023high,bollerslev2025forecasting}, (ii) return prediction with large predictor sets \citep{chinco2019sparse,gu2020empirical,ait2025predictable,aleti2025intraday}, (iii) factor/characteristic selection and SDF estimation for the cross-section of expected returns \citep{feng2020taming,freyberger2020dissecting,kozak2020shrinking,chen2025cross}, and (iv) implementable portfolio formation and asset allocation \citep{ao2019approaching,ding2024statistical}, among others.
perturbations.perturbed: Second, we contribute to the rapidly growing literature on high-dimensional problems and statistical learning in finance. As modern financial markets feature a vast set of assets, characteristics, and candidate factors, the penalized regression methods have been widely applied to (i) covariance/correlation matrix estimation and forecasting, (ii) return prediction with large predictor sets, (iii) factor/characteristic selection and SDF estimation for the cross-section of expected returns, and (iv) implementable portfolio formation and asset allocation. However, our paper is the first to suggest that high-dimensional methods are inapplicable to financial time series due to the lack of sparsity in asset returns.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the paper suggests high-dimensional methods are 'inapplicable' due to a 'lack of sparsity.' This contradicts the entire premise of the paper, which develops a high-dimensional penalized method specifically for financial time series and finds 'persistently sparse factor structures' in its empirical analysis.

perturbations.original: Third, our work contributes to the statistics literature on varying-coefficient models. Varying-coefficient models extend classical linear regression by allowing coefficients to vary over time or with other variables \citep{cleveland1991local,hastie1993varying,fan1999statistical}, which provide a flexible and interpretable way to capture dynamic covariate effects, and have been widely adopted in longitudinal studies \citep{hoover1998nonparametric,huang2002varying,huang2004polynomial}. Work on model selection in varying-coefficient models has closely tracked advances in penalized regression. For example, \citet{wang2008variable} and \citet{wang2009shrinkage} adopt the smoothly clipped absolute deviation (SCAD; \citealp{fan2001variable}) and the adaptive LASSO \citep{zou2006adaptive}, respectively; \citet{wei2011variable} combine spline-based estimation with the group LASSO \citep{yuan2006model} in high-dimensional settings; and \citet{xue2012variable} employ the nonconvex TLP to avoid reliance on a consistent initial estimator, which can be problematic for the SCAD and LASSO approaches, as the irrepresentable conditions for the initial LASSO estimator are often implausible in high-dimensional settings \citep{zhao2006model,bach2008consistency}. Our contribution to this literature is to establish an infill asymptotic theory for spline-based estimation and model selection in a nonstandard high-frequency setting. Moreover, our asymptotic framework allows for substantially weaker assumptions on the time-varying coefficients. Whereas the classical varying-coefficient literature typically requires smoothness conditions on the coefficient functions, we allow the coefficients to evolve as sample paths of It{\^o} semimartingales with infinite-activity jumps, and hence to be substantially rougher.
perturbations.perturbed: Third, our work contributes to the statistics literature on varying-coefficient models. Our contribution to this literature is to establish an infill asymptotic theory for spline-based estimation and model selection in a nonstandard high-frequency setting. Moreover, our asymptotic framework requires substantially stronger assumptions on the time-varying coefficients than the existing literature. Whereas the classical varying-coefficient literature typically allows for rough sample paths, we require the coefficients to be perfectly smooth, non-stochastic, and constant over the entire observation interval, excluding the possibility of It{\^o} semimartingales.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the paper requires 'stronger assumptions' and 'perfectly smooth, non-stochastic' coefficients. This contradicts the paper's actual claim that it allows for 'substantially weaker assumptions' and permits coefficients to be 'rougher' sample paths of 'It{\^o} semimartingales with infinite-activity jumps.'
